skill.name: verification-quality-assurance
skill.description: Verification and quality assurance guidance for truth scoring, code checks, reporting, rollback workflows, and CI quality gates.
skill.summary: Guide AI agents through verification checks, truth scoring, quality reports, and rollback-aware code review workflows.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command-execution, network, filesystem, environment, and heuristic findings. Manual review found the file is documentation with command examples, not executable code, so most command and weak-cryptography hits are false positives. The skill still carries medium operational risk because copied examples can run rollback commands, publish metrics to external services, and place API keys in command lines.

medium_findings.title: Destructive rollback command examples
medium_findings.description: The skill documents rollback workflows and a manual git reset command. These are legitimate quality-control examples, but an agent or user could lose local work if they run them without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines clearly show rollback and reset commands. The context is troubleshooting documentation, so the risk is operational rather than confirmed malicious.

medium_findings.title: External metrics export examples can disclose data
medium_findings.description: The monitoring examples pipe verification output to external endpoints, including a DataDog URL that receives an API key through an environment variable. This is expected for monitoring integration, but it can expose project metrics or secrets if copied into an untrusted environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Network posting and DD_API_KEY usage are explicit in the documentation. No hardcoded secret is present, which lowers severity from high to medium.

low_findings.title: Command execution findings are documentation examples
low_findings.description: The many npx, git, jq, and shell snippets are inside Markdown examples for verification, reports, CI, and troubleshooting. They are not executable skill code, but they should be treated as commands requiring user approval.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is a Markdown guide and the commands appear in fenced examples. There is no executable script or code path that automatically invokes them.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography detections are numeric threshold text
low_findings.description: Static findings for weak cryptography appear to match version numbers, thresholds, scores, or exit-code examples instead of cryptographic functions. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, DES, or similar weak cryptographic code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations discuss quality thresholds and exit codes. Manual review did not find cryptographic algorithm use at those locations.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance detections are benign workflow references
low_findings.description: The network reconnaissance and system reconnaissance hits correspond to monitoring integration text and git status troubleshooting. No evidence found of stealth scanning, host enumeration, or unauthorized discovery intent.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content describes security criteria and checking repository status. It lacks scanning flags, target enumeration, or exfiltration behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell pipelines to external services
dangerous_patterns.description: Verification output is piped to curl commands that post to third-party or custom endpoints. Review endpoint trust and data sensitivity before using these examples.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands explicitly post generated metrics to external URLs. The pattern is documented integration guidance, not hidden behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Rollback and hard reset commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide includes rollback commands and a manual hard reset. These commands can modify or discard repository state if run on a live worktree.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The destructive behavior is inherent to rollback and hard reset commands. The surrounding text presents them as troubleshooting examples.

content.value_statement: AI coding agents can produce changes that look complete but still fail quality, safety, or reliability checks. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured verification workflow with truth scores, reports, and rollback guidance.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to inspect truth scores across files, agents, tasks, and time periods.
Provides command examples for verification checks, batch checks, watch mode, and report generation.
Documents threshold strategies for development, staging, and production quality gates.
Shows CI examples for GitHub Actions and GitLab pipelines that fail on low scores.
Describes rollback workflows for failed verification results and last-good states.
limitations: Requires Claude Flow and a compatible project environment to run the documented commands.
Does not include executable verification code inside this skill file.
Rollback examples can discard work if used without backups or review.
Metrics exported to external services may contain sensitive project details.

use_cases.title: Add verification to pull requests
use_cases.description: Use the CI examples to require a minimum truth score before merging code changes.

use_cases.title: Monitor agent quality over time
use_cases.description: Track truth scores by agent, period, and task to identify regressions in generated output.

use_cases.title: Recover from failed generated changes
use_cases.description: Use rollback guidance to preview and revert changes that fail verification thresholds.

prompt_templates.title: Check current quality
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a project state.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the verification quality assurance skill to review current truth scores and summarize the main quality risks.

prompt_templates.title: Verify a focused change
prompt_templates.prompt: Verify the files I changed against a 0.95 threshold. Report failed checks, likely causes, and recommended fixes before any rollback.

prompt_templates.title: Design a CI quality gate
prompt_templates.scenario: Pipeline setup for a team repository.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a CI verification plan with truth-score thresholds, report artifacts, failure behavior, and manual review steps.

prompt_templates.title: Investigate recurring low scores
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced trend analysis across agents and tasks.
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze low truth-score patterns by agent, task type, and time period. Propose experiments to improve reliability without hiding failures.

output_examples.input: Review a pull request that recently failed verification.
output_examples.output: The assistant summarizes the failed score, names the affected checks, explains likely causes, and recommends targeted fixes before merge.

output_examples.input: Prepare a weekly quality report for AI-generated code.
output_examples.output: The assistant produces a concise report with trend direction, top failure categories, rollback frequency, and next actions for the team.

output_examples.input: Decide whether to roll back a generated refactor.
output_examples.output: The assistant compares the failed checks with the risk of reverting, suggests a dry run, and lists files that need manual review.

best_practices: Run verification in dry-run or reporting mode before using rollback commands.
Use stricter thresholds for production paths and lower thresholds for experimental work.
Review exported metrics for secrets or sensitive project details before sending them externally.
anti_patterns: Do not treat a high truth score as a replacement for human review on critical code.
Do not run hard reset or rollback commands on uncommitted work without a backup.
Do not send verification output to third-party endpoints without confirming endpoint ownership and data policy.

faq.question: Does this skill run verification automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflow instructions and command examples that require a configured Claude Flow environment.

faq.question: Can it be used with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The marketplace metadata lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What is a truth score?
faq.answer: A truth score is a documented quality metric from 0.0 to 1.0 used to judge output reliability.

faq.question: Is automatic rollback safe?
faq.answer: Rollback can be useful, but it can also discard work. Use previews, backups, and manual review.

faq.question: Can this skill help with CI pipelines?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes GitHub Actions and GitLab CI patterns for quality gates and reports.

faq.question: Does it detect every security issue?
faq.answer: No. It describes verification checks and should be combined with tests, scanners, and human review.
